text.title: GitHub Actions — Security Hardening Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules including a `skip-this-methodology` rule. Each pairs a binary check with sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Third-party actions MUST be pinned to a specific commit SHA, never a tag. `uses: actions/checkout@v4` is rejected; `uses: actions/checkout@<sha>` is required.
rule.rationale: Tags are mutable; a compromised tag = compromised workflow. SHA pinning is the only audit-friendly form.

rule.statement: Cloud authentication MUST use OIDC with a trust policy scoped to the repo + branch + workflow. Long-lived access keys in secrets are rejected.
rule.rationale: Static keys leak; OIDC issues per-run tokens with scoped trust, eliminating the leak class.

rule.statement: `permissions:` block MUST be declared at job or workflow scope with explicit minimum scopes. Implicit default (write-all) is rejected.
rule.rationale: Default GITHUB_TOKEN has broad write; scoping it caps blast radius if a step is compromised.

rule.statement: `pull_request_target` workflows MUST NOT check out the PR branch and execute its code with secrets. The combination is the most common GHA supply-chain exploit class.
rule.rationale: The pattern lets a PR from a fork execute arbitrary code with repo secrets — historically exploited multiple times.

rule.statement: Repository MUST run `actionlint` (or equivalent) as a required check on workflow file changes. Manual review alone is insufficient.
rule.rationale: actionlint catches expression-injection, missing permissions, and other patterns that humans miss.

rule.statement: Skip when (a) repo internal-only with no external PRs AND no secrets in workflows AND no cloud-auth; OR (b) prototype repo with no production path.
rule.rationale: Below threshold the overhead exceeds risk; above threshold the methodology is non-optional.
